# Copilot instructions

This repository contains the website content for Seven Hills Wrestling Club, a static site built with Eleventy and Markdown. All content is written in Markdown files. The guidance below reflects best practices from the [Microsoft Writing Style Guide](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/style-guide/welcome/) and the [Google Developer Documentation Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/).

## Voice and tone

- Write in a warm, friendly, and conversational tone that feels approachable to athletes, parents, and coaches.
- Be direct and clear. Say what you mean without extra words.
- Don't be overly formal or stiff, but also don't be flippant or use slang.
- Sound like a helpful, knowledgeable person, not a corporate document.

## Language

- **Second person**: Address the reader as "you." Avoid "the user," "the athlete," or third-person constructions when writing to the audience.
- **Active voice**: Prefer active voice over passive voice. Write "We offer three programs" rather than "Three programs are offered."
- **Present tense**: Use present tense whenever possible. Write "We train three days a week" rather than "We will be training three days a week."
- **Contractions**: Use common contractions (it's, you're, we'll, don't) to sound natural and conversational.
- **Plain language**: Choose familiar, everyday words over technical or formal alternatives. Write "start" not "commence," and "help" not "facilitate."
- **Conciseness**: Cut words that add length without adding meaning. Keep sentences and paragraphs short.
- **No jargon or buzzwords**: Avoid vague corporate language. Be specific and concrete.
- **No Latin abbreviations**: Write "for example" instead of "e.g." and "that is" instead of "i.e." If you need to include a clarification or example, spell it out.

## Headings

- Use **sentence case**: Capitalize only the first word of a heading and any proper nouns. Do not capitalize every word.
  - Correct: `## Coaching approach`
  - Incorrect: `## Coaching Approach`
- Keep headings short and descriptive. A heading should tell the reader what the section is about.
- Do not end headings with a period, colon, or other punctuation.

## Lists

- Use lists for three or more parallel items. For one or two items, use a sentence instead.
- Use parallel grammatical structure for all items in a list. If the first item starts with a verb, all items should start with a verb.
- Use an unordered list (hyphens) when order does not matter. Use a numbered list when steps must be followed in sequence.
- Do not punctuate list items unless they are complete sentences.

## Numbers

- Spell out numbers zero through nine: "three sessions," "five athletes."
- Use numerals for 10 and above: "12 weeks," "25 athletes."
- Use numerals for measurements, ages, and specific quantities regardless of size: "2 hours," "8 years old."

## Punctuation

- Use the **Oxford comma** (serial comma) in a list of three or more items: "stance, movement, and takedowns."
- Use one space after a period, not two.
- Use an em dash (—) to set off a phrase or add emphasis. Do not use two hyphens (--) as a substitute.

## Links

- Use descriptive link text that explains where the link goes. Do not use "click here," "here," or "learn more" as the only link text.
  - Correct: `[Contact the club](/contact/)` or `[view the full schedule](/programs/)`
  - Incorrect: `[Click here](/contact/)` or `[here](/programs/)`

## Images

- Every image must include **descriptive alt text** that communicates the content and purpose of the image to someone who cannot see it.
- Alt text should describe what is happening in the image, not just its subject. Write "Wrestler executing a double-leg takedown during a match" rather than "wrestling photo."
- If an image is purely decorative and adds no informational value, use an empty alt attribute (`alt=""`).

## Inclusive language

- Use gender-neutral language. Write "athletes" or "wrestlers" instead of "guys" or gendered terms.
- Avoid language that assumes physical ability or uses disability as a metaphor (for example, avoid "lame," "blind spot" in a figurative sense, or "crazy").
- Be welcoming to all skill levels, backgrounds, and ages in the way content is framed.

## Markdown formatting

- Use `#` for the page title (one per page), `##` for section headings, and `###` for sub-section headings.
- Use hyphens (`-`) for unordered list items.
- Wrap inline code and technical values in backticks.
- Do not add trailing spaces or extra blank lines between sections.
